questions.question: Johannes Gutenberg's invention of the printing press with movable type around 1440 transformed European society. Which was the first major book printed on his press?
questions.explanation: Gutenberg printed approximately 180 copies of the Latin Bible (the Vulgate) between 1452 and 1456 — now known as the Gutenberg Bible. The printing press reduced the cost and time of producing books dramatically, enabling the rapid spread of ideas. It made the Protestant Reformation possible (Martin Luther's 95 Theses spread through Europe within weeks), advanced science, and literacy rates across Europe.
questions.memory_hook: Gutenberg's press printed the Bible around 1454 — and then spread Luther's 95 Theses across Europe in weeks. The printing press democratized knowledge the way the internet did 550 years later.

questions.question: Charlemagne (Charles the Great) was crowned Emperor of the Romans by Pope Leo III on Christmas Day in which year?
questions.explanation: Pope Leo III crowned Charlemagne Holy Roman Emperor on December 25, 800 CE in Rome. Charlemagne united much of Western and Central Europe under Frankish rule — the largest European empire since Rome. He promoted education, standardized weights and measures, and required schools throughout his empire. He is often called the 'Father of Europe.' He was illiterate but employed scholars and promoted literacy for others.
questions.memory_hook: Charlemagne was crowned Holy Roman Emperor on Christmas 800 CE — uniting more of Europe than anyone since Rome. Ironically, the 'Father of Europe' who promoted literacy could barely read himself.

questions.question: The Boston Tea Party (December 16, 1773) saw American colonists dump 342 chests of tea into Boston Harbor. What were they protesting?
options: Taxation without representation — taxes imposed by Britain without colonial representation in Parliament
A ban on importing American-made goods to Britain
Forced quartering of British soldiers in colonial homes
Britain's refusal to allow expansion west of the Appalachians
questions.answer: Taxation without representation — taxes imposed by Britain without colonial representation in Parliament
questions.explanation: The Tea Act (1773) allowed the British East India Company to sell tea directly to colonists, actually lowering prices — but still collecting a tax. Colonists objected not to the price but to Parliament's right to tax them without their consent. The Sons of Liberty, disguised as Mohawk Indians, dumped 342 chests (46 tons) of tea worth £10,000. Britain responded with the Coercive (Intolerable) Acts, accelerating the path to revolution.
questions.memory_hook: The tea was actually cheaper with the tax — colonists weren't protesting the price, they were protesting Parliament's right to tax them at all. 'No taxation without representation.'

questions.question: Pelé scored over 1,000 career goals and won the World Cup three times. In which year did he first win the World Cup, at just 17 years old?
questions.explanation: Pelé (Edson Arantes do Nascimento) scored 6 goals in the 1958 World Cup in Sweden, including a hat-trick in the semifinal and 2 in the final, leading Brazil to its first title at age 17. He went on to win again in 1962 and 1970. He scored an estimated 1,283 goals in his career (including unofficial matches). He died on December 29, 2022, at age 82.
questions.memory_hook: Pelé won the World Cup at 17 in 1958, scoring 6 goals including 2 in the final. He'd win it twice more, making him the only player in history to win three World Cups.

questions.question: Wilt Chamberlain scored 100 points in a single NBA game on March 2, 1962. Against which team did he achieve this record?
questions.explanation: Wilt Chamberlain scored exactly 100 points for the Philadelphia Warriors against the New York Knicks in Hershey, Pennsylvania. He made 36 of 63 field goals and 28 of 32 free throws. The game had no TV coverage, and only one known photo of the event exists showing Chamberlain holding a '100' sign. The NBA record for second-highest single-game scoring is 81 points — Kobe Bryant in 2006.
questions.memory_hook: Chamberlain's 100-point game in 1962 had no TV coverage and only one photograph — but it happened. The second-highest ever is 81 (Kobe Bryant). No one has come within 19 points since.

questions.question: The 1942 film 'Casablanca,' set during WWII, starred Humphrey Bogart and Ingrid Bergman and won which major award?
questions.explanation: Casablanca won three Academy Awards including Best Picture at the 1944 ceremony. It was filmed with a script written day by day, as the actors didn't know how it would end. The film is famous for quotable lines including 'Here's looking at you, kid' and 'We'll always have Paris.' The American Film Institute rated it the third-greatest American film ever made.
questions.memory_hook: Casablanca was filmed without a finished script — actors didn't know the ending until shooting. It won Best Picture and produced some of the most quoted lines in film history.

questions.question: Bob Marley popularized reggae music globally. From which Caribbean island was he from?
questions.explanation: Robert Nesta Marley was born in Nine Mile, Saint Ann Parish, Jamaica in 1945. He formed the Wailers in 1963 and achieved international fame in the 1970s with albums like Catch a Fire, Exodus, and Kaya. Songs like 'No Woman No Cry,' 'Redemption Song,' 'Get Up Stand Up,' and 'One Love' made him the face of reggae and Rastafarian culture globally. He died of melanoma in 1981 at age 36.
questions.memory_hook: Bob Marley took Jamaican reggae to the world in the 1970s before dying of melanoma at 36. 'One Love' and 'Redemption Song' are among the best-selling songs in music history.

questions.question: J.R.R. Tolkien published 'The Lord of the Rings' in three volumes in 1954-55. In which fictional world is it set?
questions.explanation: Tolkien set The Lord of the Rings in Middle-earth — a world he created over decades, complete with languages (Elvish, Dwarvish), histories, and maps. The trilogy follows hobbit Frodo Baggins and a Fellowship questing to destroy the One Ring and defeat the Dark Lord Sauron. Peter Jackson's film trilogy (2001-2003) won 17 Academy Awards, including Best Picture for Return of the King — the first fantasy film to win it.
questions.memory_hook: Tolkien invented complete languages (Elvish, Dwarvish) for Middle-earth before writing the stories. Peter Jackson's films won 17 Oscars — Return of the King swept all 11 it was nominated for.

questions.question: The Persian Gulf is bordered by Iran to the north and contains the world's largest proved oil reserves. Approximately what percentage of the world's proven oil reserves are in the Persian Gulf region?
questions.explanation: The Persian Gulf region contains approximately 48-55% of the world's proven oil reserves, primarily in Saudi Arabia (world's largest at 17%), Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, UAE, and Qatar. The Strait of Hormuz at the Gulf's mouth is the world's most important oil chokepoint — about 20% of global oil supply passes through it daily. Control of Persian Gulf oil has been a driving factor in Middle East geopolitics since the 1940s.
questions.memory_hook: About 55% of the world's proven oil reserves sit around the Persian Gulf. The Strait of Hormuz is so critical that blocking it would immediately affect oil prices and economies worldwide.

questions.question: The Colorado River carves the Grand Canyon over millions of years. Into which body of water does the Colorado River eventually flow?
questions.explanation: The Colorado River flows 2,330 km from the Rocky Mountains of Colorado through Utah, Arizona, Nevada, and California before crossing into Mexico and reaching the Gulf of California. However, due to massive water withdrawals for agriculture, cities, and power, the Colorado River rarely reaches the sea anymore — the last substantial flow to the Gulf was in 1998. The river now essentially runs dry 100 miles from the coast.
questions.memory_hook: The Colorado carved the Grand Canyon over 6 million years. Now it barely reaches the sea — so much water is diverted for cities and farms that it often runs dry 100 miles from the Gulf of California.

questions.question: What is natural selection, as described by Charles Darwin in 'On the Origin of Species' (1859)?
options: The deliberate breeding of plants and animals by farmers
The process by which organisms with heritable traits better suited to their environment tend to survive and reproduce more, over time changing species
A god's selection of which species should survive
Random genetic mutations that improve an organism's appearance
questions.answer: The process by which organisms with heritable traits better suited to their environment tend to survive and reproduce more, over time changing species
questions.explanation: Darwin's key insight: traits vary in populations, some traits help survival/reproduction, offspring inherit traits, so helpful traits accumulate over generations. Given enough time and environmental pressure, populations change — new species emerge. Darwin developed the theory on the Galápagos Islands, noticing that finches had different beaks suited to different foods. Wallace independently reached the same conclusion, prompting Darwin to finally publish.
questions.memory_hook: Natural selection: vary, inherit, survive, repeat over millions of years. Darwin sat on the theory for 20 years until Wallace independently discovered it — then finally published in 1859.

questions.question: The carbon cycle describes how carbon moves through Earth's systems. Which human activity has most disrupted the carbon cycle in the past 200 years?
options: Burning fossil fuels, which releases carbon stored underground for millions of years back into the atmosphere
questions.answer: Burning fossil fuels, which releases carbon stored underground for millions of years back into the atmosphere
questions.explanation: Fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas) are the compressed remains of ancient organisms that stored carbon underground for millions of years. Burning them rapidly returns this carbon to the atmosphere as CO₂. Before industrialization, atmospheric CO₂ was ~280 ppm; it now exceeds 420 ppm. This 50% increase traps more heat in the atmosphere (greenhouse effect), warming the planet and disrupting climate systems.
questions.memory_hook: Fossil fuels are ancient carbon stored underground for millions of years. Burning them returns it all to the atmosphere in decades — raising CO₂ from 280 to 420+ ppm in 200 years.
